coredata.dc:title: Dispersing carbon dots in non-polar rubber by slurry compounding and in situ compatibilizing
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Peng
Jin, Guangzhi
Wang, Wencai
Wang, Runguo
Guo, Baochun
Zhang, Liqun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained increasing attention in the elastomer composites due to their unique physical and chemical properties. Nevertheless, due to high hydrophilicity of most kinds of CDs, it is difficult to achieve a fine CDs dispersion within the non-polar rubbers by traditional melt milling, especially when the CDs loading is high. To tackle this problem, we propose a simple and cost-effective strategy, i.e., the combination of slurry compounding and in-situ compatibilizing, to prepare the rubber/CDs composites. The CDs slurry was simply prepared by mixing the CDs powder with water. Subsequently, the slurry along with the compatibilizer (polybutadiene grafted with maleic anhydride, BR-g-MAH) were compounded with rubber via open milling. The resulted rubber composites exhibited significantly improved mechanical properties. More importantly, the CDs endowed the rubber with exceptional antioxidative property. We envisage that this scalable yet environmentally-friendly strategy may pave the way for industrial-scale utilization of CDs in the elastomer and beyond.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rubber
Carbon dots
Slurry compounding
Compatibilization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs slurry and rubber composites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51790501 , 51825303 and 51673013 ), the Basic Science Center Program of NSFC ( 51988102 ), the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2017YFB0306900 and 2017YFB0306904 ), and the Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectrum for CDs. (b) TEM image and size distribution (inset) for CDs, (c) CDs dry powder, (d) CDs slurry (the solid content is 66.7 wt%), (e) The CDs slurry was dropwise added into the rubber compound during the two-roll mixing process.
The comparison of morphologies of the rubber/CDs composites prepared slurry compounding with or without in situ compatibilization.

Mechanical performances of the rubber composites. (a) Drying milling composites, (b) Slurry compounding composites, (c) Plots of δ* versus λ-1 for the slurry compounding composites. (d) Comparison of the reinforcing effect of CDs and silica toward SBR.
(a) UV–vis spectra of salicylic acid-Fe2+-H2O2 aqueous solution within the CDs concentration from 0 to 0.5 mg/mL. (b) Comparison of antiaging performance of SBR/30CDs/5B and SBR/30silica composites.
The formulations of the rubber composites a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Rubber ingredients: ZnO (zinc oxide), 5 phr; stearic acid, 2 phr; CZ (N-cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazole sulfinamide), 1.5 phr; DM (dibenzothiazole disulfide), 0.5 phr; Sulfur 1.5. Antioxidant 4020 refers to N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N′-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine; Si69 refers to bis [3-(triethoxysilyl)propyl] tetrasulfide.

head.ce:title: Dispersing carbon dots in non-polar rubber by slurry compounding and in situ compatibilizing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering
State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Materials and Engineering, South China University of Technology, 381 Wushan Road, Tianhe District, Guangzhou, 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Materials and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Polymer Materials and Engineering, South China University of Technology, 381 Wushan Road, Tianhe district, Guangzhou, 510640, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained increasing attention in the elastomer composites due to their unique physical and chemical properties. Nevertheless, due to high hydrophilicity of most kinds of CDs, it is difficult to achieve a fine CDs dispersion within the non-polar rubbers by traditional melt milling, especially when the CDs loading is high. To tackle this problem, we propose a simple and cost-effective strategy, i.e., the combination of slurry compounding and in-situ compatibilizing, to prepare the rubber/CDs composites. The CDs slurry was simply prepared by mixing the CDs powder with water. Subsequently, the slurry along with the compatibilizer (polybutadiene grafted with maleic anhydride, BR-g-MAH) were compounded with rubber via open milling. The resulted rubber composites exhibited significantly improved mechanical properties. More importantly, the CDs endowed the rubber with exceptional antioxidative property. We envisage that this scalable yet environmentally-friendly strategy may pave the way for industrial-scale utilization of CDs in the elastomer and beyond.

ce:section.ce:para: Rubbers have been widely acknowledged as strategically important materials as these materials with high entropic elasticity are indispensable in many important areas such as tires, seals and damping materials [1,2]. Generally, the reinforcing fillers are necessary for rubbers to improve their mechanical properties [3–5]. Also, for a real application, the antioxidant is indispensable for rubbers, especially diene rubbers, to improve the long-term reliability and prolong the service life. Unfortunately, the toxicity of most antioxidants and the blooming tendency of antioxidants have plagued people for long time. In addition, the production processes for traditional antioxidants are complicated and eco-unfriendly [6]. In this regard, it is highly desirable to develop novel reinforcing filler with intrinsic antioxidant capability and low toxicity for rubbers.

Carbon dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional nanodots, have attracted increasing attention, owing to their unique physical and chemical properties, such as tunable photoluminescence property, low toxicity, high reinforcing effect, and high antioxidative property. Due to the ease of preparation and functionalization, the CDs have been used for diverse functional materials [7–9]. Extensive efforts have also been devoted to incorporating the CDs as a kind of novel filler for polymer composite, and significant progresses have been witnessed in this field. Polymer/CDs composites can be made by a large range of processing techniques (depending on the type of polymer matrix) [10,11], they can be fabricated by (i) blending polymers with CDs, for example blending the PVA solution and CDs. (ii) In situ synthesis of polymer matrix in the presence of CDs, i.e., CDs are incorporated into the polymerizable precursor solution to obtain a homogeneous mixture. Then the system can be triggered polymerization to obtain polymer/CDs composites. (iii) one-step synthesis of CDs/polymer composites. i.e., the CDs and polymer matrix are simultaneously generated in one system, during which the CDs are in-situ assembled into polymer matrix. Rubber is a special polymer with unique flexibility, and the CDs have attempted to be used as novel fillers for rubber [6,12,13]. However, up to now, compared to other carbonaceous fillers (such as carbon black [14], carbon nanotubes [15], graphene [16,17]), the potential of CDs in the elastomer is far from being fully exploited. The properties of the rubber/CDs composites crucially depend on the dispersion state of CDs and rubber-CDs interfacial interactions. Except for the thermodynamic compatibility between rubber and CDs, the processing technique may have significant impact on the dispersion and interface of the composite. Previously, the CDs have been incorporated into the rubber matrix by two ways. One is the CDs-rubber latex compounding [12,13], in which the CDs solution is mixed with rubber latex to achieve a fine dispersion within the rubber matrix. However, the latex compounding method is difficult to be scaled up, as it involves the utilization of large amount of water and time-consuming steps. In addition, not all rubbers have the latex form. The other method is the melt mixing technique by incorporating the dried CDs powder into the rubber matrix by shearing force in an internal mixer or open mill. Considering the industrial manufacturing, the melt processing is obviously the most promising technique for the rubber/CDs composites. Nevertheless, the huge challenge is the tendency of ultrafine CDs to agglomerate due to the high surface energy of CDs, and the high viscosity of rubber gum. When the CDs loading is high, the agglomeration of CDs within rubber matrix is expected to be more pronounced, which may lead to limited reinforcing efficiency.

A possible way to achieve a fine CDs dispersion within the rubber matrix during melt mixing is the utilization of the un-dried CDs. We accordingly propose the slurry compounding route. We firstly disperse the CDs in a liquid media to form a CDs slurry, then blend the slurry with the rubber. Indeed, the slurry compounding method is a kind of modified melt compounding method, and the essence of the slurry compounding is the liquid-feeding of filler in the melt-mixing process. The filler is deliberately swell/dissolved in a suitable solvent to overcome the self-aggregation during the compounding process, because the solvent could effectively depress the interaction among the filler particles. So far, the slurry compounding technique has already been utilized for the fabrication of different rubber based composites such as rubber/clay composite [18], rubber/boron nitride composite [19], and rubber/graphene composite [17,20]. In the present work, we attempt to implement the slurry compounding method in the preparation of rubber/CDs composites for the first time. Because of the excellent water solubility of CDs, it is obviously that the water is the most suitable processing medium to prepare the CDs slurry. The water could dissolve/swell the CDs and therefore weaken the interactions among CDs particles, which is essential and beneficial to promote the CDs dispersion within the rubber matrix. The water in the system can be effectively removed during the oven-drying and exhaust of hot-pressing processing. The preparation of the rubber/CDs composites by slurry compounding is schematically illustrated in Fig. S1.

The compatibility between hydrophilic CDs and commonly used diene rubbers, such as natural rubber (NR) and styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) is obviously poor. We therefore select the commercially available polybutadiene grafted with maleic anhydride (BR-g-MAH) to in situ modify the interfaces during the slurry compounding processing. BR-g-MAH is an ideal compatibilizer for the rubber/CDs composite as the backbone of BR-g-MAH could co-vulcanize with the rubber matrix while the anhydride groups of BR-g-MAH are ready to interact with the amine-passivated CDs [21]. The results demonstrated that the slurry compounding strategy along with the introduction of BR-g-MAH could simultaneously improve the CDs dispersion and CDs-rubber interfacial adhesion, leading to a significantly improved mechanical properties of the composites. More importantly, the CDs could endow the rubber composites with excellent antioxidative ability. The high reinforcing effects and antioxidative ability are correlated to the improved dispersion and interface of the composites and the unique antioxidative capability of the CDs.

Styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) with trade name of SBR1502 (styrene content of 23.5 wt%) was supplied by Jilin Chemical Industry Co., Jilin, China. Precipitated silica (Ultrasil VN3, specific surface area of 175 m2/g) was purchased from Degussa Chemical. BR-g-MAH (trade name of Ricobond 1731, the number averaged molecular weight of 5500, MAH content of 17 wt%) was purchased from Cray Valley Company, France. Citric acid monohydrate was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd., China. 1,2-Ethylenediamine was purchased from Aladdin Reagent, China. The rubber additives were purchased from local sources. All the chemicals were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs slurry and rubber composites
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of CDs slurry: We firstly prepared the amine-passivated CDs following the protocol described elsewhere [6,22,23]. In detail, amine-passivated CD is shown as follows, 7.7 g of citric acid monohydrate was dissolved in 70 g KH2PO4 aqueous solution (0.002 mol/L), and then 2.2 g ethylenediamine was added slowly into the mixture solution drop by drop under stirring condition. Subsequently, the homogeneous mixture solution was heated under 700 W microwave irradiation for 9 min in a microwave oven, during which the transparent liquid gradually became a red-brown solid, indicating the formation of raw CDs. Then 2 mL water was mixed with the raw CDs, and the as-prepared mixture was washed and precipitated with 500 mL ethanol for 2 times. The obtained CDs were dissolved in 500 mL H2O and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles. After that, the CDs aqueous solution were subjected to a reduced pressure distillation to remove water, and the obtained CDs was washed and precipitated with 500 mL ethanol again. Finally, the purified CDs were vacuum dried at 60 °C. Taking advantage of the amine-passivated CDs having excellent solubility in water, we prepared the CDs slurry by simply mixing the CDs powder and water (weight ratio of CDs to water in the slurry is 2:1).

Preparation of rubber composites: The SBR was chosen as the model rubber matrix as it is the most widely used synthetic rubber. The detail of slurry compounding is described as follows. The weight ratio of CDs to water in the slurry is 2:1, and the amount of CDs slurry in the formula refers to the weight of CDs in the slurry. A given amount of CDs slurry with/without BR-g-MAH was firstly well mixed with SBR by a two-roll mill. After that, to remove the water in the system as much as possible, the obtained rubber compounds were oven dried at 80 °C for 10 h. The dried rubber compounds were then mixed with the sulfur-based curing package by the two-roll mill. Lastly, the well-mixed rubber compounds were compression molded at 160 °C for optimum cure time determined by a vulcameter. For comparison, the dried CDs powder with/without BR-g-MAH were directly incorporated into the rubber composites following the conventional rubber processing procedure. The SBR/silica composites (silica 30 phr) with/without 4020 was also prepared. The detail of the formulations (weight parts per 100 parts of SBR) are summarized in Table 1 .

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer GX spectrophotometer with KBr pellets scanning from 4000 to 400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by using PHI-5300 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an Al Kα radiation source. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigation was performed on a JEM-3010 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) with an acceleration voltage of 300 kV. Prior to the measurement, the sample was prepared by dropping a droplet of diluted CD aqueous solution onto a copper grid and drying. SEM investigations of the rubber samples were conducted on a HITACHI SU-8020 SEM (HITACHI Co., Japan). The samples were plated with a thin gold layer of before any observations. Tensile tests of rubber were carried out at room temperature using CMT4104 electronic tensile tester (SANS, China) according to ASTM D 412. The strain rate was 500 mm/min. Swelling study was conducted as follows. The rubber samples were immersed in toluene for 72 h. Swelling ration is defined as follows:
Q =
Swollen weight − Dried weight
Original weight × α
Here α is the mass fraction of the rubber in the composites.

The capability of CDs to scavenge free radical produced by Fenton reaction was investigated following the protocol reported elsewhere [23]. By detecting the CDs-induced variation of the absorption peak at 510 nm for the salicylic acid/Fe (Ⅲ) complex, the radical scavenging capacity of CDs can be evaluated [24]. The absorption spectra of the mixture solutions were obtained by a PerkinElmer Lambda 950 spectrophotometer.

Fig. 1 a shows the FTIR spectrum of the amine-passivated CDs, the N–H stretching vibration (3100-3500 cm-1) is observed. Particularly, the absorptions at 1654 cm-1 and 1560 cm-1 are attributed to CO stretching and N–H blending vibration of amide group, respectively [6,22]. According to the XPS result (Fig. S2), three types of N-related bonding can be identified in the deconvoluted N 1s spectrum of the CDs. The peaks at 399.6, 400.3, and 401.5 eV are assigned to the amine groups, amide-carbonyl groups, and graphitic N, respectively [25–27]. The FTIR and XPS results clearly demonstrate the presence of –NH2 and amide groups on the surface of CDs. The morphology of the CDs was studied by the TEM observation (Fig. 1b, S3). As shown, most primary CDs particles had a spherical shape with the diameter around 3 nm. The dry CDs powder is a kind of yellowish fluffy powder (Fig. 1c), which could mix with water to form a CDs slurry (Fig. 1d). The CDs slurry could easily be successfully compounded into the rubber by conventional two roll-milling (Fig. 1e).

The amine-passivated CD is nano-sized particle that has a high density of amino groups on the surface. As a consequence, the CDs particles would interact with each other and form micrometer sized irregular agglomerates (Fig. S4a). Therefore, it is very difficult to disaggregate the CDs agglomerates into primary particles in a nonpolar rubber matrix by the shearing force during the two-roll milling. Expectedly, a serious agglomeration is observed (Fig. S4b). In addition, one can also easily observe the sharp boundary between the CDs phase and SBR, demonstrating the poor compatibility between CDs and SBR. We then utilize the BR-g-MAH to in-situ modify the interfaces between the CDs and the rubber during the drying milling process. Although the boundary between CDs and SBR is blurry (Fig. S4c), the large CDs agglomerations are still observed, which is mostly probably attributed to the fact that the BR-g-MAH compatibilizer just slightly improved the thermodynamic compatibility between the CDs and SBR, and the interfacial adhesion is not strong enough to largely promote the CDs dispersion within SBR matrix. When the CDs were added into the rubber matrix as fluid (slurry) instead of dry powder, a unique micron-sized spherical CDs aggregate could be clearly identified (Fig. 2 a, 2b). This may be attributed to the fact that the CDs nanoparticles have a strong tendency for self-association upon drying from CDs slurry as the re-aggregation of filler is thermodynamically favorable [28,29]. In addition, it is observed that the spherical CDs aggregates are pull out from the matrix, indicating the poor interfacial adhesion between polar CDs phase and nonpolar SBR.

We then utilize the BR-g-MAH to in-situ modify CDs during the slurry compounding process. As shown in Fig. 2c and d, a much better CDs dispersion can be achieved. For example, the CDs were dispersed in nanoscale to submicro scale (50–200 nm) in S-SBR/15CDs/5B and S-SBR/30CDs/5B composites. It is obvious that the BR-g-MAH facilitates the CDs dispersion. One can image that the water in the slurry can effectively decrease the interactions among adjacent CDs particles. Simultaneously, BR-g-MAH could effectively interact/wrap the CDs particles during the slurry compounding process, thereby preventing the fine CDs particles from fusing together again, thus leading to an improved CDs dispersion within the rubber matrix.

Although the achieved dispersion of CDs (50–200 nm) is still much large than for the primary CDs particles (2–5 nm), this technology combining slurry compounding and in situ compatibilization realize, for the first time, the fine dispersion of highly hydrophilic and highly polar CDs in nonpolar rubber in a facile and scalable manner. We then investigated the effects of compounding methods on the mechanical properties of the rubber composites. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the CDs powder shows negligible reinforcing effect towards SBR through conventional dry-milling method. With in situ compatibilization, the SBR/15CD/5B still exhibits unsatisfactory mechanical performance. This result is reasonable as the direct compounding CDs powder leads to severe agglomeration of CDs.

S-SBR/15CDs/5B and S-SBR/30CDs/5B composites exhibit much better mechanical performances than for their counterparts without BR-g-MAH. For instance, the tensile strength values for S-SBR/15CDs/5B and S-SBR/30CDs/5B are increased by 2 times and 3.2 times comparing to S-SBR/15CDs and S-SBR/30CDs, respectively (Fig. 3b). In addition, and the fracture energy (calculated from the area under the stress−strain curve before rupture) for S-SBR/15CDs/5B and S-SBR/30CDs/5B are increased by 160% and 240%, comparing to S-SBR/15CDs and S-SBR/30CDs, respectively. To gain insight into the compatibilizing effect of BR-g-MAH on the tensile performances of the rubber composites, the stress-strain curves are converted into Mooney-Rivlin plots (Fig. 3c) as follows.
δ* = δ/(λ - λ-2) = 2C1 + 2C2 λ-1
where δ is the applied stress, λ is the extension ratio, and C1 and C2 are constants which are independent of λ. The equivalent stress (δ*) is a linear function of reciprocal of the extension ratio (1/λ). As shown, all the curves exhibit a“U” shape, the upturn of Mooney-Rivlin plot indicates the occurrence of rubber chain orientation bridging nearby reinforcing particles, and it could be used to evaluated the interaction between the rubber and filler. Usually, the upturn occurs at a lower λ (corresponding to higher λ-1) in the curves indicates a stronger interfacial adherence between the filler and rubber matrix [30–32]. As shown, the value of λ-1 at which the upturn occurs increases upon the addition of BR-g-MAH, demonstrating the interfacial interactions between CDs and rubber matrix are improved. We therefore conclude that the reinforcing efficiency of CDs toward the elastomer is significantly improved by the combination of slurry compounding and in situ compatibilization strategy. We further compared the mechanical performances of the SBR/30CDs/B with SBR/30silica composites with/without antioxidant 4020 (Fig. 3d). Explicitly, the mechanical performance of S-SBR/30CDs samples is comparable to those achieved by SBR/30silica composites.

We further used the swelling experiments to study the rubber-filler interaction of the composites. As shown in Fig. S5, Q is the amount of solvent absorbed by 1 g of rubber and subscripts f and g refer to filled and gum vulcanizates. As expected, the value of Qf/Qg for S-SBR/15CDs/5B and S-SBR/30CDs/5B are lower than for S-SBR/15CDs and S-SBR/30CDs, respectively. This can be interpreted as follows. (i) The CDs dispersion in S-SBR/15CDs/5B and S-SBR/30CDs/5B are much better than for their counterparts without BR-g-MAH, (ii) BR-g-MAH could improve the rubber-filler interfacial bonding. The stronger interfacial interaction and better dispersion of CDs in S-SBR/CDs/B composites could effectively depress the mobility of rubber chains and therefore delay the diffusion of solvent molecules into the composites. The swelling study further substantiated that the rubber-filler interaction in S-SBR/CDs/B composites are stronger than that in S-SBR/CDs composites.

Thermal oxidative aging is the most common form of rubber aging. Diene-based rubbers with unsaturated carbon-carbon bonds are vulnerable to thermal/oxygen ageing due to the oxidative scission of unsaturated chains and sulphidic crosslinks, which would lead to reduction in the mechanical properties of composites [33]. Therefore, the development of a diene-based rubbers with high ageing resistance is desirable. Amine-passivated CDs possess a large amount of surface moieties, including –OH, –NH2, and –CONH− groups. These groups with proton-donating ability and the sp2 hybridized carbon core with high electron affinity would contribute excellent quenching ability of reactive radicals [23]. As shown in Fig. 4 a, we introduce CDs with various concentration from 0 to 0.5 mg/mL into the salicylic acid-Fe2+-H2O2 system. The H2O2 is decomposed into O2 and H2O under the catalyst of iron ion which cycles between Fe2+ and Fe3+ (The detailed reaction mechanism of Fenton reaction is shown in Supporting Information). Salicylic acid (SA) is introduced to detect Fe(III), as Fe(III) could complex with SA. The resulting Fe(III)/SA complex exhibits deep color and has obvious UV absorption around 530 nm in the UV–vis spectra. The peak absorption around 530 nm decrease gradually with increasing of CDs concentration (Fig. 4a), which is due to the fact that the CDs could effectively scavenge the reactive oxygen radical and reduces the content of Fe(III)/SA complex [6]. These results indicate that the CDs can be used as a potential antioxidant for polymers. We accordingly evaluate the antioxidant performance of rubber/CDs composites. As shown in Fig. 4b, it is obvious that SBR/30CDs/5B exhibits much higher retentions of tensile strength and elongation at break after 3 or 6 days thermal-aging testing compared to SBR/30silica and SBR/30silica/4020 composites. For instance, the tensile strength retention and elongation retention for S-SBR/30CDs/5B after ageing for 6 days is 92.7% and 74%, respectively, which are much better than the tensile strength retention (51.5%) and elongation retention (45.4%) of SBR/30silica/4020 composite. These results explicitly demonstrate that the CDs could used as a kind of promising reinforcing filler with intrinsic anti-oxidative capability for elastomers. And we believe that the rubber/CDs composite can be a promising high anti-aging material in tire, seal, electrical cable, etc.

Aiming to achieve fine dispersion of the CDs in the non-polar rubbers in a facile and scalable manner. We attempted to use the combination of slurry compounding and in situ compatibilizing strategy to prepare the SBR/CDs composites. During rubber compounding, the presence of water molecules in the slurry could break the CDs agglomerations, and simultaneously the BR-g-MAH could effectively interact/wrap the CDs particles, thereby preventing the fine CDs particles from fusing together again. Moreover, the addition of BR-MAH could effectively enhance the interfacial bonding between the non-polar SBR and polar CDs. Consequently, the well-dispersed CDs could confer the rubber with the combination of high mechanical properties and high antioxidative properties. We envisage that the combination of slurry compounding and in-situ compatibilizing strategy might provide new opportunities for the preparation of high-performance elastomer/CDs composites and beyond.

Peng Yu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Guangzhi Jin: Data curation, Investigation. Wencai Wang: Investigation. Runguo Wang: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Baochun Guo: Writing - review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Liqun Zhang: Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51790501, 51825303 and 51673013), the Basic Science Center Program of NSFC (51988102), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFB0306900 and 2017YFB0306904), and the Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.coco.2020.100429.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A real recycling loop of sulfur-cured rubber through transalkylation exchange of C–S bonds

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design of next-generation cross-linking structure for elastomers toward green process and a real recycling loop

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Elastomer reinforced with regenerated chitin from alkaline/urea aqueous system

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A method to improve the mechanical performance of styrene-butadiene rubber via vulcanization accelerator modified silica

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High performance luminescent thermosetting waterborne hyperbranched polyurethane/carbon quantum dot nanocomposite with in vitro cytocompatibility
